Abstract
Tool-integrated reasoning (TIR) augments large language models (LLMs) with
the ability to invoke external tools, such as search engines and code interpreters,
to solve tasks beyond the capabilities of language-only reasoning. While rein-
forcement learning (RL) has shown promise in improving TIR by optimizing final
answer correctness, existing approaches often overlook the efficiency and cost
associated with tool usage. This can lead to suboptimal behavior, including exces-
sive tool calls that increase computational and financial overhead, or insufficient
tool use that compromises answer quality. In this work, we propose Optimal Tool
Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), a simple yet effective RL-based
framework that encourages models to produce accurate answers with minimal
tool calls. Our method introduces a tool-integrated reward that jointly considers
correctness and tool efficiency, promoting high tool productivity. We instantiate
this framework within both Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Group Rel-
ative Preference Optimization (GRPO), resulting in OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO.

Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive reasoning capabili-
ties when fine-tuned via reinforcement learning (RL) with simple rule-based rewards. This has led
to the emergence of large reasoning models (LRMs) - such as OpenAI’s o1 [1], DeepSeek-R1 [2],
and QwQ [3] - which exhibit creative and diverse behaviors in extended Chain-of-Thought (CoT)
reasoning [4]. These models develop meta-reasoning capabilities reminiscent of human cognition,
such as reflection and decomposition, which have been shown to enhance performance on com-
plex tasks [5–7]. However, the internal reasoning capacity of LRMs alone is often insufficient
or even infeasible for tasks that require interaction with external environments, such as accessing
up-to-date domain knowledge or performing precise computations. To overcome these limitations,
tool-integrated reasoning (TIR) has emerged as a promising paradigm [8–12], enabling LLMs to
interface with external tools like search engines, calculators, or code interpreters. By bridging
language understanding with external capabilities, TIR significantly expands the problem-solving
scope of LLMs beyond language-only reasoning.

such as reflection and decomposition, which have been shown to enhance performance on com-
plex tasks [5–7]. However, the internal reasoning capacity of LRMs alone is often insufficient
or even infeasible for tasks that require interaction with external environments, such as accessing
up-to-date domain knowledge or performing precise computations. To overcome these limitations,
tool-integrated reasoning (TIR) has emerged as a promising paradigm [8–12], enabling LLMs to
interface with external tools like search engines, calculators, or code interpreters. By bridging
language understanding with external capabilities, TIR significantly expands the problem-solving
scope of LLMs beyond language-only reasoning.
While recent efforts have sought to extend reinforcement learning (RL) to tool-integrated reasoning
(TIR) by leveraging rule-based rewards tied to final answer correctness [13–15], these approaches
fall short in guiding models to use tools judiciously. Despite achieving strong performance and
improved generalization, current methods often encourage indiscriminate tool usage, introducing
critical challenges during both training and inference. On the one hand, frequent and unnecessary
tool calls incur substantial computational and temporal overhead, as executing tools—especially
at scale—demands significant infrastructure and increases operational costs, limiting deployment
feasibility. We also observe a pattern of cognitive offloading [16], in which the model extensively relies
on external tools, thereby limiting the development and utilization of its internal reasoning capabilities.
This issue becomes more pronounced as model size increases. On the other hand, optimizing solely
for answer accuracy neglects the inherent costs (e.g., time, financial) and effectiveness of individual
tool usage, which may lead to inefficient reasoning behaviors. In particular, models may excessively
rely on external tools even when fewer or no tools are actually needed (i.e., Tool Overuse) [11], or
conversely, avoid using tools altogether even when external assistance is crucial (i.e., Tool Underuse)
[17]. Both patterns hinder optimal reasoning and highlight the need for more refined training
objectives - ones that balance correctness with efficient and contextually appropriate tool usage.
We aim to develop such a method based on a foundational assumption: for each question and each
model, there exists an optimal number of tool calls, defined as the minimal number required for the
model to arrive at a correct answer. Crucially, this optimal number is not fixed across models or
questions. For the same question, models with different reasoning or representation capabilities may
require different levels of tool assistance. Similarly, even for a single model, the number of necessary
tool calls can vary significantly depending on the complexity of different question. For example,
some questions may be solvable through internal reasoning alone - requiring zero tool calls2 - while
others may necessitate one or more tool interactions to yield a correct answer. This observation
highlights the limitations of supervised fine-tuning (SFT), which typically enforces a uniform policy
across diverse model-question pairs. In contrast, reinforcement learning (RL) provides a more flexible
framework, enabling reward signals to be dynamically adjusted based on each model’s behavior and
tool usage. Ideally, among all tool-integrated reasoning trajectories that lead to the correct answer for
the same question, the reward function should assign the highest reward to the trajectory that uses
the optimal (i.e., minimal) number of tool calls. Other correct trajectories that involve excessive tool
calls - by definition, suboptimal - should be rewarded less3. This formulation allows the model to
learn policies that jointly optimize for both answer correctness and efficient, context-aware tool use
on a per-question, per-model basis.
We propose Optimal Tool Calls controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), a simple yet effective
RL-based method that enables large language models (LLMs) to learn precise and adaptive control
over external tool usage. OTC-PO trains models to optimize the number of tool calls required to reach
a correct solution, thereby reducing both training and inference costs without sacrificing accuracy. To
achieve this, we introduce a tool-integrated reward that modulates traditional reward signals, such
as correctness, with a scaling coefficient reflecting tool efficiency. This encourages the model to
prioritize correct answers that require fewer tool invocations. Our formulation shifts the optimization
objective from correctness alone to tool productivity, defined as the ratio between task benefit (e.g.,
answer accuracy) and tool usage cost (e.g., number of tool calls). To the best of our knowledge, this is
the first RL-based framework that explicitly optimizes for both the efficiency and effectiveness of tool-
integrated reasoning. OTC-PO is lightweight and broadly applicable, requiring only minimal changes

We propose Optimal Tool Calls controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), a simple yet effective
RL-based method that enables large language models (LLMs) to learn precise and adaptive control
over external tool usage. OTC-PO trains models to optimize the number of tool calls required to reach
a correct solution, thereby reducing both training and inference costs without sacrificing accuracy. To
achieve this, we introduce a tool-integrated reward that modulates traditional reward signals, such

the first RL-based framework that explicitly optimizes for both the efficiency and effectiveness of tool-
integrated reasoning. OTC-PO is lightweight and broadly applicable, requiring only minimal changes
to standard RL pipelines - just a few lines of code - making it easy to adopt in existing systems. We
validate our approach across two common tool modalities: web search and code execution. Using
multiple LLMs—including Qwen-2.5-3B/7B-Base and Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B/7B-Base, we show that
OTC-PO significantly reduces tool calls during reasoning trajectories while maintaining comparable
accuracy to conventional approaches. In summary, the key contributions are as follows:

• We are the first to i) systematically address the tool efficiency problem via RL, which has
been largely overlooked in prior research; ii) identify the cognitive offloading phenomenon
in TIR of LLMs; iii) introduce the concept of tool productivity to measure the effectiveness
and efficiency of TIR.
• We propose a simple, scalable, and generalizable OTC-PO algorithm to encourage the model
to use optimal tool calls to solve the problem and maximize the tool productivity, building
on top of a fundamental observation that there is an optimal number of tool calls for each
question and model pair. We note that it is compatible with various RL algorithms and can
be easily implemented with only a few line changes.
• We implement OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO as two typical methods without losing the
adaptability and generalization, and the experimental results on several benchmarks and
baselines demonstrate significant reductions in tool call cost while preserving most of the
accuracy in both in-domain and out-of-domain evaluation.
2 Related Work
Tool Utilization for LLMs. Teaching LLMs to use tools enables them to interact with external
environments while overcoming several inherent limitations such as restricted access to up-to-date
or domain-specific knowledge and poor mathmatical operation capabilities. There are three major
methods which can achieve this goal: 1) prompting engineering [18–23], which guides the model’s
behavior through carefully designed input templates or few-shot examples without modifying model
weights; 2) supervised finetuning on tool-integrated reasoning datasets [24, 8, 25, 26, 11], where the
model learns from annotated trajectories that demonstrate when and how to use tools in context; and
3) reinforcement learning [13, 14, 27, 28], which allows the model to directly learn tool-use strategies
by interacting with an environment and optimizing long-term rewards, enabling more adaptive and
goal-directed behaviors. While prompting and supervised fine-tuning have shown promising results,
they rely heavily on expert-designed prompts and tool-integrated annotations, which limits their
scalability and generalization. As a result, recent efforts have shifted toward reinforcement learning,
which demonstrates improved performance and generalization through simple rule-based rewards.
Tool-integrated Reasoning Efficiency. Only few of studies from prompting engineering and
supervised fine-tuning attention on tool-integrated reasoning efficiency issues in terms of the cost of
tool usages during the reasoning [23, 17, 11]. In detail, Wang et al. [23] first propose a prompting-
based framework: Self Divide-and-Conquer (SelfDC) to leverage the self-aware confidence score
of LLMs to decide whether or not need to call tools during reasoning, achieving better trade-off
between effectiveness and efficiency in the context of RAG. Furthermore, several works follow this
direction and explore more boarder applications and larger tool spaces [11, 17, 29]. For example,
SMART [11] collect the well-designed dataset to finetune the model to only call tools when the
knowledge is outside the inherent parametric knowledge of LLMs. Despite these advancements,
most existing approaches still rely on complex prompt engineering or annotated datasets, which
hinders their adaptability and scalability to new scenarios. In contrast, the efficiency of tool-integrated
reasoning within reinforcement learning frameworks remains largely underexplored.
Reward Shaping in RL. Reward shaping plays a critical role in reinforcement learning for tool-
using language models, as it directly shapes the behavior the model learns to optimize [30, 31].
Recent advances have introduced several reward signals for LLMs to consider the correctness and
the language-only reasoning efficiency together such as the length of responses and difficulty of the
questions [32–34]. For instances, Length Controlled Policy Optimization (LCPO) [32] is proposed
to satisfy the length constraints while optimizing reasoning performance and some efforts try to
dynamically allocate inference time compute based on task complexity [33, 35].
• We are the first to i) systematically address the tool efficiency problem via RL, which has
been largely overlooked in prior research; ii) identify the cognitive offloading phenomenon
in TIR of LLMs; iii) introduce the concept of tool productivity to measure the effectiveness
and efficiency of TIR.

question and model pair. We note that it is compatible with various RL algorithms and can

accuracy in both in-domain and out-of-domain evaluation.

3.3 OTC-PO: Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization
Instead of only considering the correctness (and optionally format) of the answer, we argue it
is important to consider the effectiveness and efficiency of tool-integrated reasoning trajectory.
Specifically, different questions may require different numbers of tool interactions depending on
the internal capabilities of model, and for each question–model pair, there exists an optimal (i.e.,
minimal) number of tool calls needed to reach the correct answer. Given the real-world cost and
latency associated with tool calls, it is crucial to move beyond coarse, answer-only rewards and
instead provide finer-grained signals that capture the overall quality of TIR trajectory.
To this end, we propose Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), can be easily
integrated with any RL algorithms, and is broadly applicable across different models and settings,
aiming to preserve overall accuracy while significantly reducing the cost of tool interactions. At the
heart of OTC-PO is a reward mechanism that ranks the current number of tool calls
m

n

for the same question. This approach enables the model to learn efficient tool use without requiring
prior knowledge of the optimal tool budget. Nonetheless, the framework can be naturally extended

4 Experiments
4.1 Set up
Datasets and Baselines. We mainly follow the Search-R1 [13] and ToRL [14] setting and use
same baselines and datasets for the fair and comprehensive evaluation. Specifically, we use NQ and
HotpotQA as training dataset for search, and we use the dataset provided in ToRL for code. We also
directly compare our methods with several baselines such as SFT, Base-RL, retrieval-augmented
generation baselines (i.e., RAG, IRCoT) and more importantly the Search-R1 and ToRL themselves.
Evaluation Metrics. Besides the exact match (EM) and the average tool calls (TC), we additionally
define a new metric, tool productivity (TP), which measures the effectiveness and efficiency of tool
calls during inference. Specifically, TP is defined as the number of correctly answered questions
per unit of tool call:

provide correct answer, but how economically it arrives at that correctness.

5.1 Tool Use Behavior Analysis
Training. Figure 3 shows the training behaviors of different methods. Our method not only achieves
comparable results with fewer tool calls and shorter responses, but also enables faster and more
efficient training optimization. This is particularly important, as it significantly reduces the time and
cost associated with real-time tool interactions during training, both in terms of financial expenses
and computational overhead.

using fewer tool calls, whereas the baseline fails to answer correctly (C). Table 3 shows the results of

of test cases (i.e., A). When also considering cases where our

correct answers with optimal tool calls. Unlike prior work that primarily focuses on final answer

and efficiency of tool usage, thereby promoting tool-use behaviors that are both smart and cost-
effective. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that explicitly addresses tool-use
efficiency in TIR from a RL perspective, and our method offers a scalable and principled solution
to make LLMs more capable and economical agents in multiple situations and benchmarks. We

6 Conclusion
In this work, we introduce Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), a simple yet
effective reinforcement learning framework that explicitly encourages language models to generate
correct answers with optimal tool calls. Unlike prior work that primarily focuses on final answer
correctness, our approach incorporates a tool-integrated reward that accounts for both effectiveness
and efficiency of tool usage, thereby promoting tool-use behaviors that are both smart and cost-
effective. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that explicitly addresses tool-use
efficiency in TIR from a RL perspective, and our method offers a scalable and principled solution
to make LLMs more capable and economical agents in multiple situations and benchmarks. We
emphasize that this is an ongoing project, and we plan to release additional results, analyses, and
extensions in future versions. We hope our work can serve as a foundation for further exploration
into efficient and scalable tool-integrated reasoning with language models.

spans.content: Table 5: Analysis of tool overuse and underuse of OTC-PO against Search-R1. The size of evaluation
datasets is NQ (3,610), HotpotQA (7,142), TriviaQA (10,852), PopQA (12,347), 2Wiki (12,576),
Musique (2,292), Bamboogle (64).
C Analysis
C.1 Tool Overuse v.s. Tool Underuse
We define two metrics: i) the number of test cases that our method can provide same answer with
baseline but using less tools (i.e., LT); and ii) the number of test cases that our method can provide
correct answer by using more tools while the baseline can not answer it correctly (i.e., MT). Table 5
shows the results. It is observed that smaller LLMs tend to underuse tools, while larger LLMs tend
to overuse them when reinforcement learning optimization focuses solely on answer correctness.
Specifically, we find our method not only can use less tools to arrive the same answer with the
baseline but also can use more tools to provide correct answers, especially on small LLMs. When the
model size get larger, it is found that MT always is 0, serving as another evidence that large LLMs
over-rely on external tools even when it can not provide correct answer.
